sentences.text: Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The little girl, who was related to me, was [NON-ENGLISH].
Her first name, I-- I don't remember.
It's left my mind.
And the other one was Purim.
The first name-- one second.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the mother was Simka Rosensweig.
One daughter is alive in Israel, [?
Malke. ?] And the little sister, I don't remember her name.
With-- with these other women.
It wasn't across the river.
It was by the river, on the same side.
But police always guard the rivers, because they knew that people will run away.
But they didn't shoot, maybe the fog.
Yeah, the Purim is Faigela.
I think Faigela Purim, that was her name.
And we came to that Christian man.
And he says, I will take you in.
But it's no place.
Better sit in the garden.
And go on the way to Wiczowka.
We heard when we were in the-- in ghetto that there is a Misiura.
He was out crying.
But the Germans were looking for him.
And he used to send signals to the ghetto that the young people should go out.
And if they have ammunition, they should bring.
And they we're going to see-- try to do something.
But you know, they were afraid.
If they leave, they can come and kill the family.
So they were standing or sitting and waiting for their death.
So we knew that we have to find Misiura.
We knew from what town he was.
He was from Wiczowka.
So we asked the way, how to go there.
He direct us.
They direct us.
And we came there.
We came there.
And we were sitting a couple days.
Then the runaways, who run away from ghetto, eventually, you looked for Misiura, too.
So somebody came.
My brother came with another guy.
They were on the other side.
So we were already our family.
And the-- the other people, who came over, but we were still-- Then, you know, maybe in a week later, Misiura.
How did you survive those few days?
Where did you get food?
You go to the-- to the gardens.
It was still-- you take food.
And then we took-- made-- made like a-- a pot from-- from the-- what you-- you find in the gardens, when you--
Yeah.
Not a pail.
A-- I don't know how to say.
My mind doesn't work so.
In Yiddish it's a [YIDDISH], you know, the-- with a yoder inside.
And whatever.
And we survived.
Then Misiura came and find us.
And he says, you stay in this place.
Don't go nowhere.
And when-- when some young men will come, we'll try to do something to protect you.
In the meantime, he used to bring some food or a few--
It was-- he was there.
And he knew that those bushes or those forests were safe.
And people will come there.
So he came.
And he was a good man.
And he-- he helped in this way.
Then when Purim-- what I said, the sister.
He had hidden, somewhere, a-- a gun.
So he went, at night, to town.
The town was already clear of Jews, because, when we ran away, the-- the next day, they took everybody, and they put it in that grave.
So they went.
They brought the gun.
So they had already a gun.
And somehow-- somehow, the Russian soldiers run away from the-- when they were defeated, they went to the bushes to be safe.
So they joined.
A few of them, they joined.
Between them was a Jewish guy.
His name was Alek Kabugow.
And when we saw a Jewish man with a rifle, and he'll going to protect us, it was like God, even more than that.
And he did protect us.
Misiura was good to us.
And they start to go, you know, to-- against the-- the-- those people who worked with the Germans.
And they--
A few of them, they killed.
Like Purim, he knew who killed his father.
He went to town.
He went in.
He said, that's for my father.
And he has already the gun.
At that time, I didn't have a job.
We were just-- we're just hiding.
If-- if we were cold, we made a fire.
And if we had some potatoes, we baked potatoes.
Slept on the ground.
Put some-- put some the leaves and there we slept.
And you know what?
When you are a while in the cold, you don't feel the cold.
And they used to bring this old stuff, with-- and you cover yourself.
And that's how you live.
Now, you don't realize how you could do that.
But that's how it was.
You couldn't go to somebody's house, even off a-- on a farm.
Because if they catch them, they'll kill them.
They-- they were not-- you couldn't ask for it.
They'll give you food.
But they'll say, go away.
They'll give you something to wear.
But they couldn't hide you.
Some of them did but very, very few.
And that's how we lived.
We lived there maybe six months like that.
Then the-- Misiura had a bigger, bigger crowd, so they took us five girls to cook for them.
Or they brought some wounded.
Always when you go, you-- you-- you fight the Germans, you bring-- you bring wounded people.
So we helped.
We were there maybe about three or four months.
And then one of them was anti-semitic.
I don't recall his name.
And he said that he-- he-- we should go back to the-- to the other Jewish people.
And they took in the local girls to help out.
Because some of the local people joined.
So they had sisters or whoever.
I don't remember exactly how that happened.
So the Jewish boys, like Alek and Purim, they were discarded of that group.
And they fought, some of them, a lot of them.
But I knew they got killed in those operations, even to go for food.
And why do you do that?
If-- if-- if that the way, we're going to leave your place-- your-- and we're going to go somewhere else.
Because those people were-- there were a lot of underground groups.
So they wouldn't take in women, but people like that, they more than-- than-- than glad to take.
And they came to the-- and they said, you can't do things like that.
We are just like-- What the German did, they did.
But now it's a different ballgame.
So then there was a Jewish doctor they kept, because when they brought the-- the-- the wounded, you need somebody.
And he was-- he ran away from somewhere.
I don't know from where he was.
But he was there.
He came and he said to-- he said to the boys, you come back.
Misiura said it's OK.
Whatever, he made a mistake.
But I don't know if you should come or not.
Because they can call you back, and they can kill you for the desertion.
You know?
I think you better leave the-- the place, for a while, until it'll settle.
So when they start to leave, they said, well, we're going to leave.
But they can come and kill you.
Because-- because of you, we left.
You better come with us.
And you-- you know, whatever it's going to be, it's going to be.
So we ran away. with them.
That's when I left my brother and sister.
Because I-- I-- I couldn't take them with me.
And I didn't know where I'm going.
So there was no use to-- here they are around even farmers who knew them, who-- you know, who helped them.
So we ran away.
And the next day, the Ukraine says that they promised them their country, the German promised them, when they were going to defeat.
So there were [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know who was their-- but they-- we called them [NON-ENGLISH].
Maybe-- maybe the-- the-- the leader was [?
Bulwa. ?] I don't know.
And they came.
When they used to get-- they knew the-- the bushes.
The Germans didn't know.
But they knew.
And when the Germans came, they-- they took them.
And the partisans, they were fighting those [NON-ENGLISH], too, a lot.
So by-- so by that time, we left.
And Misiura had to go somewhere.
Germans came close.
And they had to go somewhere.
So the Ukrainian came in, and they killed, from our town, maybe 40 or 50 people.
But we weren't there.
The night before, we ran away.
But my sister and brother was there.
And they killed that Simka, with her daughter, with one of her daughters and a son, in that-- in those 50 people, and more and more, all from my town.
My brother survived with my sister.
And they collected whoever they found.
Like it was my three cousins, who lived through all that, and they were killed by them.
He found them with another guy.
He was in New York when they buried them.
And they left, too.
But I didn't know where they are-- where-- what happened to them.
But you know, we-- and we went to another forest.
And we were there.
And whoever came along, I used to ask, where is this and where is that?
So somebody told me that they survived.
But they don't know where they are.
So then I hear that Rowne is free.
Pinsk wasn't free.
Pinsk-- by Pinsk, they were a long time, the Russian, with the-- with the German, they were fighting.
But they couldn't take the city.
But Rowne was-- When was this?
That was-- in '40, in '43-- in '44.
In '44, the beginning of '44, Rowne was free.
So--
No.
Mostly we were among our own people.
We were with my own friends from school, from home.
You know, so we were like a family.
It wasn't-- it-- it wasn't like we are between strange people.
Well, when you're young-- the-- the older one didn't survive.
Whoever survived, the Ukrainian killed them there.
And from our town, because we were close to the forest, we survived more than the big cities.
The big cities, thousands of people, they survived very few.
And we went out to the-- to the forest.
We were maybe together, maybe over 100, 130.
And those Ukrainian mostly killed.
When we were freed, maybe 50 was alive, a few in Israel, a few here, and, in New York, two families, that's all.
Yeah.
Well, I-- I was raised that way.
And I-- I would never leave.
When I-- when I was freed, I wasn't religious.
But I looked for a-- I looked for kosher meat.
I used to-- I brought up the kids, they should be-- they should have kosher homes.
In the forest, whatever you got, you cooked.
And you-- you never looked at it.
We didn't know once.
We knew it Yom Kippur, so we didn't eat.
We didn't eat.
And one of the guys baked some potatoes was said, it's ready.
Oh, you know what, I wasn't hungry.
But other times, I didn't know what it what.
Well, there, you-- you don't keep religion.
You find even a piece of-- anything you find, you eat.
It was your survival.
And sure, you-- you weren't religious.
But yet, when you're free, you can't go away from it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And when we came to Rowne, I find my sister and my brother.
And we were there a year.
Rowne was free.
But they used to bomb-- put bombs on the city.
So at night-- at night, they used to put the bombs.
So at night, we used to go out from the city, sleep outside.
Because they-- a few of them got killed in the bunkers.
And in the daytime, we used to return to the city.
No.
In Rowne, I didn't work, but my brother worked.
He-- he's handy.
He fixed shoes.
He did all kinds of things.
And you know.
But probably, by that time, they gave out some food to those people.
I don't remember exactly how it were, but we weren't hungry already, being in Rowne.
And one helped each other, a lot.
Well, there was no Jews left.
There was no-- no-- no--
That survived?
There was-- you have a good feeling for those who were good.
And you had a bad feeling for those who were bad.
Like those-- this-- those five bad people, who used to go at night, and do whatever they want, Misiura found a way to persuade them, they should come in to his-- to his place.
And he worked-- worked with them.
I don't know if he got them this way, or he got them the other way.
But he-- he brought three, the two brothers and the police-- the kommandant, the-- the-- they were higher than they were from--
One was Dr.
Yuk's brothers, the two, Dr.
Yuk brothers, the two.
And one was-- I don't remember his name, but he was the kommandant.
He was-- when-- when the Germans was there.
And he brought them.
He says, yeah, you will go with us.
And I'm going-- you'll work with us.
He brought them before the-- before the place where they were, before the camp.
And they said like that, he-- to Purim.
And-- and to him, he said, it's enough to kill innocent people.
It's enough to rape Jewish girls, kill them.
And Purim and Alek, I think-- I wasn't there-- killed those three, you know?
So he-- they took revenge of those three people.
There were more but on those.
One ran away to Australia.
Because a lot of them ran away with the-- with the Germans.
And a couple of years ago, they called from Australia if-- what happened?
If I knew Ivanechko?
I says, sure.
We used to be afraid for him.
We used to hide from him.
He used to kill.
And you know what? 30 Christian people came to Australia to give evidence that they saw how they killed this and this, the woman and child, and by name.
And you know, they freed him.
They-- he's free in Australia.
My brother went there.
My husband didn't let me go.
He says, well, your brother is going.
It's enough.
He didn't let me go.
So he-- he ran away to Australia.
He lived so many years in comfort.
And that's how it was.
And he's free now.
Mind you, before the-- the court, he tried to kill himself.
But he only-- he only-- he survived.
And then, when he got better, they put him on-- and they freed him, like others they freed.
What can you do?
And you lived for about a year in Rowne?
Yeah.
And then the Jewish-- the-- we hear that you can go to Israel.
But you can't go to Russia.
You have to go to Poland.
And being Polish citizens, we register.
And we went.
We came to Bytom.
And from Bytom, the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a [NON-ENGLISH].
And they send us as Greek refugees.
We're not supposed to talk Polish.
We don't understand.
We're Greek refugees.
When they stopped us on the border, where are you going?
We were 10 in a party.
And we went with another family but-- from our town.
It was [?
Erdmann, ?] he-- a father, two sons, and a sister.
And we came to-- we ran through all the borders.
When we talked between us, we talk a little bit Hebrew, which the authority didn't understood.
And just, they talked to us Polish.
We just-- they gave us a document that we are Greek refugees.
And we came to Linz.
And in Linz, in Bindermichl, they-- it was a camp.
They gave us a room.
They gave us food.
And-- and I had family in Los Angeles.
So there, through somebody, I found the numbers.
They used to send us whatever, parcels.
And there came papers to go to Los Angeles, to my aunt and uncle.
But from the union here, from Toronto, it came a delegation that would take some tailors.
And my husband worked before the war as a tailor.
They really did-- they really wanted to take out some-- some people from there.
So we came here.
In-- right after the war, in Bindermichl.
We lived on the same block.
I lived upstairs.
And he lived downstairs.
We usually, even then, you-- you lived in the same.
It was blocks.
And everybody had a room or two rooms.
Well, it probably wasn't easy.
The-- the-- the fear goes with you.
But it's easier to go from slavery to freedom than from freedom to slavery.
We are adjustable things.
Well, if-- if I would say 100% no, I-- I-- it wouldn't be true.
It affected.
But-- but your mind, you can change things.
It has to be this way.
You look forward.
And you know what somebody said in the-- that when a-- a little leaf falls, you're sorry.
But when the whole-- when the whole tree falls, then you get-- you have in common with some other people.
And they're all in the same boat.
I consider myself lucky.
I had a sister and my brother.
My husband had three brothers and a sister.
And the millions of people, they just-- thousands of people, they just survived, just by themselves.
So in a way, I was lucky, luckier than other people.
No.
No.
The [INAUDIBLE] officers killed my three cousins.
And my-- my sister had-- my mother had a sister, five girls and a son-- nobody.
In Pinsk, my brother-- my-- my father had a sister, with four sons and two daughters.
Nobody survived.
They were married already.
They had children.
The-- the-- the aunt what used to help us, she had five children.
Nobody survived-- with her husband.
The other one didn't have children.
So she got killed, with her husband.
I mean-- but we were already the lucky ones having a sister and a brother.
It wasn't-- And, you know, as I look back, I am happy I helped in any way I could.
At that time, even a meal-- when I was cooking, and somebody came, and I gave a meal, they'll never forget me.
If I ever had a an extra shirt, and I gave it to somebody, they'll never forget it.
And even here, whoever comes, they don't go to a hotel.
They come right here.
They know they're welcome.
When we-- when I come to Israel or come to New York, it's more than a sister comes to them.
Because I always had help-- gave a help-- a help as much as I could.
I was-- myself, I couldn't help.
But that you have a good feeling that you weren't selfish.
And you were-- you understand-- you understood life, what it's all about.
You saw it.
So that's--
Oh, we stayed till we came here, in 1948, in February, with General Sturgis.
Well, [LAUGHS] it's a beautiful country.
How can you not like Canada?
How could you say something?
We came.
We had everything.
We worked.
I worked, too.
My husband worked.
We had children.
My-- my children are all university graduated.
They all work as professionals.
I have four, three daughters and a son.
And they are married.
And they have children.
Well, we had some misfortunate thing with my grandson.
But like I said, life from then is not the same.
But it'll-- it'll stay with us till the end of our years.
And like I said, a couple of times, I don't think that I'll finish life that way.
But that's life.
You can't change things.
I have now 12.
One is married.
And even the grandchildren, she's a lawyer now.
She married a nice boy, a lawyer, too.
I mean it's not the profession, it's the goodness of the people.
And we are a family.
They all come.
They used to come every Friday.
Now, as I get older, it's a little bit harder.
But the holy days, they all spend here.
And they-- a very happy family, as you saw.
And we hope nothing bad would come along our away until the end.
Yeah.
